docs.title: Comprehensive Git Guide
docs.text: A practical guide to Git for teams new to version control, from installation through advanced workflows.

docs.title: Get Started
docs.text: This guide is organized into progressive sections. Choose your path:

docs.title: New to Git?
docs.text: Start with Concepts to understand what Git is and how it works, then follow through Installation and Basic Workflow.

docs.title: Visual Learner?
docs.text: Jump to the Visual Guide to see ASCII diagrams of how the commit graph evolves through various Git operations.

docs.title: Looking for a Specific Topic?
docs.text:
• Branching & Merging — Work with branches, merge code, resolve conflicts
  • Advanced Features — Rebase, cherry-pick, stash, squash commits
  • Collaboration — Pull request workflows, code review, team practices
  • Troubleshooting — Fix mistakes, recover lost work, undo commits
  • Command Reference — Quick lookup for commands and flags

docs.title: Full Learning Path
docs.text:
1. Concepts — Understand version control, repositories, commits, and the Git model
  2. Installation — Get Git running on macOS or Linux
  3. Basic Workflow — Init a repo, commit changes, push to a remote, pull updates
  4. Branching & Merging — Create branches, switch contexts, merge code, resolve conflicts
  5. Advanced Features — Rebase, cherry-pick, stash, squash commits, amend history
  6. Collaboration Patterns — Pull request workflows, code review, team practices
  7. Fixing Mistakes — Undo commits, recover lost work, reset history safely
  8. Command Reference — Quick lookup for common commands and flags
  9. Visual Guide — See how the commit graph evolves through Git operations

docs.title: Key Principles
docs.text: This guide emphasizes: - Understanding over memorization — Why Git works a certain way matters more than remembering syntax - Safe operations — We show how to undo mistakes before teaching destructive commands - Team workflows — Real-world practices that scale from solo projects to teams - Practical examples — Every concept includes a concrete example you can try

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Within a Git command, add --help: git commit --help
  • For interactive learning: git (no arguments) shows basic commands
  • If something goes wrong, jump to Fixing Mistakes

docs.title: 1. Core Concepts
docs.text: Understand what Git is and how it works before you start using it.

docs.title: What is Version Control?
docs.text:
Version control is a system that tracks changes to files over time. It answers: - Who changed what? - When was it changed? - Why was it changed? - Can we go back to an earlier version?

Without version control, you might do this: 

project_v1.zip
project_v2.zip
project_FINAL.zip
project_FINAL_REVISED.zip
project_FINAL_REVISED_ACTUALLY_FINAL.zip

With version control, you have one folder and the ability to track every change ever made.

docs.title: What is Git?
docs.text:
Git is a distributed version control system. This means: - Every copy of a Git repository (folder) is complete and independent - You can work offline and sync changes later - Multiple people can work on the same project without stepping on each other's toes

Git was created by Linus Torvalds in 2005 to manage the Linux kernel (a project with thousands of contributors). It's now the industry standard.

docs.title: Repository
docs.text:
A repository (or "repo") is a folder managed by Git. It contains: - Your project files - A hidden .git folder that stores all version history

You create a repo with git init or clone an existing one with git clone.

docs.title: Commit
docs.text:
A commit is a snapshot of your project at a specific moment in time. Think of it as a save point in a video game.

Each commit is an immutable object that contains: - Content snapshot — The complete state of every tracked file - Metadata — Author, committer, timestamp, commit message - Parent reference — Link to the previous commit (or commits in a merge) - Tree object — A hierarchical reference to all files and directories

Example: "Fix login bug where users can't reset passwords"

docs.title: How Git Stores Content
docs.text:
Git doesn't store diffs (changes); it stores complete snapshots. Here's how it works internally:

Blob: Each file is stored as a blob (binary large object) identified by its SHA-1 hash: 

File content: "Hello, world!"
SHA-1 hash:   2ef7bde608ce5404e97d5f042f95f89f1c232871

Git computes the hash of the content. If two files have identical content, they share the same blob (deduplication). Even a single character change produces a different hash and a new blob.

Tree: A directory structure is represented as a tree object that maps filenames to blob hashes: 

.git/objects/
├── ab/
│   └── cd1234...  (blob: README.md content)
├── ef/
│   └── gh5678...  (blob: src/app.js content)
└── trees/
    └── 1a2b3c...  (tree object listing files)

A tree object looks like: 

100644 blob ab cd1234...  README.md
100644 blob ef gh5678...  src/app.js
040000 tree 99 zz9999...  src/

This structure creates a hierarchy: a root tree points to blobs (files) and subtrees (directories).

Commit: A commit object ties everything together: 

tree 1a2b3c4d5e6f (the root tree snapshot)
parent abc123def456 (previous commit)
author John Doe <john@example.com> 1718000000 +0000
committer John Doe <john@example.com> 1718000000 +0000

Fix login bug where users can't reset passwords

The commit includes: - tree — Hash of the root tree object (entire project snapshot) - parent — Hash of the previous commit (creates the chain) - author — Name, email, and timestamp of who made the changes - committer — Name, email, and timestamp of who created the commit (often same as author) - message — Your commit message

This entire commit object is itself hashed, producing the commit's unique identifier (e.g., abc123def456).

docs.title: Tracking Changes
docs.text:
Git compares commits by comparing their tree objects. To see what changed between commits:

git diff abc123def456 def456abc789

Git walks both trees and compares blob hashes. If a blob hash is different, the file changed. If a blob is missing, the file was deleted. If a new blob appears, the file was added.

This is why Git can efficiently tell you exactly what changed—it's comparing hashes, not scanning file contents.

docs.title: Author and Committer
docs.text:
The commit stores two identities:

  • Author — The person who originally wrote the code. Set by you when you commit.
  • Committer — The person who created the commit. Usually the same as author, but different in scenarios like:
  • Cherry-picking a commit someone else wrote
  • Rebasing commits others wrote
  • A maintainer merging a pull request

Both include: - Name and email - Unix timestamp (seconds since January 1, 1970) - Timezone offset

Example: 

author Jane Doe <jane@example.com> 1718000000 +0000
committer maintainer <maint@example.com> 1718001000 +0000

docs.title: The Commit Chain
docs.text:
Commits form a linked chain through parent references:

[Commit 1] → [Commit 2] → [Commit 3] → [Commit 4]
 (parent)    (parent)      (parent)      (current)

Each commit points to its parent. This creates an immutable history—you can't change a commit without changing all commits that follow it (which is why rewriting history is tricky).

docs.title: Immutability and Integrity
docs.text:
Once created, a commit cannot be changed. The commit's hash is computed from its entire contents (tree, parent, author, message, etc.). If anything changes, the hash changes, making it a different commit.

This immutability gives Git its safety guarantee: history is tamper-proof. If someone modifies a commit, the hash no longer matches, and Git detects the tampering.

docs.title: Branch
docs.text:
A branch is an independent line of development. By default, you work on the main (or master) branch.

Branches let you: - Work on new features without affecting the stable code - Experiment safely - Prepare code for review before merging back in

Example: Create a feature/dark-mode branch to build a dark mode feature without breaking the main app.

docs.title: Remote
docs.text:
A remote is another copy of your repository, usually on a server. The most common remote is origin (typically on GitHub, GitLab, or your company's Git server).

Remotes let you: - Share code with teammates - Back up your work - Collaborate on the same project

docs.title: Index (Staging Area)
docs.text:
The index (or "staging area") is a holding area between your working files and a commit. It's stored as a file at .git/index.

When you change a file: 1. The change exists in your working directory (unstaged) 2. You explicitly stage it with git add, which writes the file content to the object store and updates the index 3. You create a commit with git commit, which creates a new commit object from the staged files

docs.title: How Staging Works
docs.text:
When you run git add filename.txt:

  1. Git reads the file from disk
  2. Computes its SHA-1 hash
  3. Stores the file content as a blob object in .git/objects/
  4. Updates the index file to point to that blob's hash

The index now contains: 

filename.txt → blob_hash_abc123def456
another.js  → blob_hash_def456abc123

When you run git commit, Git:

  1. Reads the index to get all staged file hashes
  2. Creates a tree object from those hashes
  3. Creates a commit object pointing to that tree
  4. Updates the current branch to point to the new commit

This two-step process lets you craft clean, logical commits. You don't have to commit every change; you can cherry-pick which changes go into each commit by selectively staging files (or parts of files with git add -p).

docs.title: Git's Object Model
docs.text: To understand how Git works, you need to know about its core objects and how they relate.

docs.title: Objects
docs.text:
Git stores everything as objects, identified by SHA-1 hashes. There are four main types:

Blob — A file's content (immutable binary data) 

Type: blob
Content: function login() { ... }
Hash: abc123def456...

Tree — A directory listing (maps names to blobs and subtrees) 

Type: tree
Entries:
  100644 blob abc123 README.md
  100644 blob def456 app.js
  040000 tree ghi789 src/
Hash: tree123def456...

Commit — A snapshot with metadata 

Type: commit
Tree: tree123def456
Parent: commit_prev_hash
Author: Jane Doe <jane@example.com> 1718000000 +0000
Committer: Jane Doe <jane@example.com> 1718000000 +0000
Message: Fix login bug
Hash: commit123abc...

Tag — A named reference to a commit (optional, for releases) 

Type: tag
Object: commit123abc
Tagger: Jane Doe <jane@example.com>
Name: v1.0.0
Message: Release 1.0
Hash: tag123def...

All objects are stored in .git/objects/ with their hash as the filename.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
References (refs) are pointers to commits. They're stored in .git/refs/ and contain a commit hash.

Branch — A reference that moves forward with each commit 

.git/refs/heads/main
Content: abc123def456... (the commit hash main points to)

When you commit on main, Git updates this file to point to the new commit.

HEAD — A special reference pointing to your current location 

.git/HEAD
Content: ref: refs/heads/main

When you're on the main branch, HEAD points to the main ref, which points to a commit.

When you do a detached HEAD (checkout a commit directly), HEAD contains the commit hash directly: 

.git/HEAD
Content: abc123def456...

Remote tracking branch — A reference tracking a remote branch 

.git/refs/remotes/origin/main
Content: def456abc123... (the commit hash origin/main points to)

When you git fetch, Git updates these refs to match what's on the remote.

docs.title: The DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)
docs.text:
Commits form a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Each commit points to its parent(s):

     main
       ↓
Commit A ← Commit B ← Commit C
           (merge)     ↙
          Commit D

Merge commits have multiple parents. Rebased commits have a linear chain. Branches are just labels pointing to commits in this graph.

This DAG structure is why Git is so powerful: - You can efficiently find common ancestors - You can detect cycles (impossible in Git—it's acyclic) - You can traverse history in multiple directions

docs.title: The Git Workflow
docs.text:
Here's how a typical workflow looks:

1. Edit files (working directory)
2. Stage changes (git add)
3. Commit changes (git commit)
4. Push to remote (git push)

And pulling updates:

1. Fetch changes from remote (git fetch or git pull)
2. Merge them into your branch (automatic with git pull)

docs.title: Key Terminology
docs.text: Term Meaning Commit A snapshot of the project at a point in time Branch An independent line of development Main/Master The default, production-ready branch Remote A copy of the repo on a server (like GitHub) Origin The default remote, usually where you cloned from Push Send your commits to a remote Pull Fetch and merge changes from a remote Fetch Download changes from a remote without merging Merge Combine changes from one branch into another Rebase Replay commits from one branch onto another (advanced) Staging Preparing changes to be committed HEAD A pointer to the current commit you're on

docs.title: Hashing and Content Addressing
docs.text: Git uses SHA-1 hashing (and more recently SHA-256) to identify objects. The hash is computed from the object's content.

docs.title: Why Hashing Matters
docs.text:
Content Addressing — Objects are found by their content hash, not a filename. The same content always produces the same hash:

Content: "Hello, world!"
SHA-1: 2ef7bde608ce5404e97d5f042f95f89f1c232871

If two files have identical content, they're stored once and referenced twice (deduplication).

Integrity — If someone modifies a commit's content, the hash changes. This makes history tamper-proof—you can verify the entire history's integrity by checking hashes.

Immutability — A commit's hash includes its content, author, timestamp, and message. Change any detail, and the hash changes. This is why you can't edit a commit without creating a new one.

docs.title: In Practice
docs.text: When you see a commit hash like abc123def456789: - The first 7 characters (abc123d) are usually enough to uniquely identify it - You can use git show abc123d to view the full commit - The full 40-character hash is the complete identifier

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Git's design solves real problems:

  • Safety — Every commit is permanent. You can't accidentally lose work.
  • Collaboration — Multiple people can work on the same project simultaneously.
  • History — You can see exactly what changed, when, and why.
  • Experimentation — Branches let you try things without risk.
  • Code Review — You can review changes before merging.

docs.title: Ready to Use It?
docs.text: Now that you understand the concepts, jump to Installation to get Git running on your machine.

docs.title: 2. Installation
docs.text: Get Git running on your machine in 5 minutes.

docs.title: Via Homebrew (Recommended)
docs.text:
If you have Homebrew installed:

brew install git

Verify installation: 

git --version

docs.title: Via Xcode Command Line Tools
docs.text:
If you're a Mac developer, the easiest option is often the Xcode Command Line Tools:

xcode-select --install

A dialog will appear. Click Install and wait for completion. Then verify: 

git --version

docs.title: Via Official Installer
docs.text:
1. Download the latest macOS installer from git-scm.com
  2. Open the .dmg file and run the installer
  3. Follow the prompts (defaults are fine)
  4. Verify: git --version

docs.title: Debian / Ubuntu
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install git

docs.title: Fedora / RHEL / CentOS
docs.text:
sudo dnf install git

Or for older CentOS: 

sudo yum install git

docs.title: Arch Linux
docs.text:
sudo pacman -S git

Verify installation on any Linux distribution: 

git --version

docs.title: Initial Setup
docs.text:
Once Git is installed, configure your identity. Git uses this for every commit you make:

git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"

Replace with your actual name and email (preferably the same email you use on GitHub or your team's Git server).

Verify your configuration: 

git config --global --list

You should see your name and email in the output.

docs.title: That's It
docs.text: You're ready to use Git. Next: Basic Workflow

docs.title: 3. Basic Workflow
docs.text: Start using Git with these everyday commands.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Create a new repository
git init

# Clone an existing repository
git clone https://github.com/username/repo.git

# Check the status of your changes
git status

# Stage changes for commit
git add filename.txt
git add .                    # Stage all changes

# Commit staged changes
git commit -m "Describe what changed and why"

# Send commits to remote
git push

# Fetch and merge changes from remote
git pull

docs.title: Option 1: Start Locally
docs.text:
Create a new folder and initialize a Git repo:

mkdir my-project
cd my-project
git init

This creates a hidden .git folder that tracks everything. You're now ready to add files and make commits.

docs.title: Option 2: Clone an Existing Repository
docs.text:
Copy an existing repo from a server (e.g., GitHub):

git clone https://github.com/username/my-project.git
cd my-project

This downloads the entire project history and sets up a remote called origin pointing back to the original repo.

docs.title: 1. Check What's Changed
docs.text:
git status

Output might look like: 

On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  modified:   README.md
  modified:   src/app.js

Untracked files:
  new_feature.txt

  • Modified — Changed files Git already knows about
  • Untracked — New files Git hasn't seen before

docs.title: 2. Stage Your Changes
docs.text:
# Add a specific file
git add README.md

# Add all changes
git add .

# Stage only parts of a file (interactive)
git add -p filename.txt

Check what you've staged: 

git status

docs.title: 3. Commit
docs.text:
A good commit message is crucial. It should explain why the change was made, not what changed (the code already shows that).

git commit -m "Fix login bug where sessions expire immediately"

Or for a longer message: 

git commit

This opens your editor. Write a summary on the first line, then a blank line, then more details.

Example: 

Fix login bug where sessions expire immediately

Users were unable to stay logged in for more than a few minutes
because the session timeout was set to 1 second instead of 1 hour.
This affected both web and mobile clients.

Fixes: #1234

docs.title: 4. Review Your Commits
docs.text:
See the commits you've made:

# Recent commits
git log

# More detailed view
git log --oneline

# With author and time
git log --pretty=format:"%h - %an, %ar : %s"

docs.title: 5. Push to Remote
docs.text:
Once you're happy with your commits, send them to the server:

git push

This sends your commits from the main branch to the origin remote.

First time pushing a new branch? Use: 

git push --set-upstream origin branch-name

Or the shorter version: 

git push -u origin branch-name

docs.title: Pull (Most Common)
docs.text:
git pull

This fetches changes from the remote and merges them into your current branch. It's the combination of two commands: 

git fetch        # Download changes
git merge        # Merge them into your branch

For most everyday work, git pull is what you want.

docs.title: Fetch (Advanced)
docs.text:
If you want to see what changed before merging:

git fetch
git log origin/main    # See what's on the remote
git merge              # Merge if it looks good

docs.title: I Made Changes and Want to Undo Them
docs.text:
If you haven't committed yet: 

git restore filename.txt        # Undo changes to one file
git restore .                   # Undo all changes

docs.title: I Committed Something Wrong
docs.text:
If it's your most recent commit and you haven't pushed yet: 

git reset --soft HEAD~1    # Undo the commit but keep changes
git reset --hard HEAD~1    # Undo the commit and discard changes

More on fixing mistakes: Fixing Mistakes

docs.title: I Forgot to Add a File to My Commit
docs.text:
If you haven't pushed yet: 

git add forgotten-file.txt
git commit --amend --no-edit    # Add to the last commit

docs.title: I Want to Save My Work Temporarily
docs.text:
git stash                   # Save your changes
# ... do something else ...
git stash pop              # Restore your changes

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Commit often — Small, focused commits are easier to review and debug
  • Write good messages — Future you will thank present you
  • Pull before you push — Avoid conflicts by staying up-to-date
  • Don't commit secrets — No passwords, API keys, or credentials in your repo
  • Use branches — Keep main stable and work on features in separate branches

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Ready to work with branches? Jump to Branching & Merging.

docs.title: 4. Branching & Merging
docs.text: Work on multiple features in parallel without stepping on each other's toes.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Create and switch to a new branch
git switch -c feature/dark-mode

# Make changes and commit
git add .
git commit -m "Add dark mode styles"

# Push the branch to remote
git push -u origin feature/dark-mode

# Switch back to main
git switch main

# Merge the feature branch in
git merge feature/dark-mode

docs.title: Why Branches?
docs.text:
Without branches, everyone works on the same copy of the code. This creates chaos: - Person A is working on a login feature - Person B is working on a search feature - Their changes conflict everywhere

Branches let each person work in isolation, then merge changes back together.

docs.title: Create a Branch
docs.text:
git switch -c feature/dark-mode

This creates a new branch called feature/dark-mode and switches to it.

Old syntax (still works): 

git checkout -b feature/dark-mode

docs.title: List Branches
docs.text:
# Local branches
git branch

# All branches (local + remote)
git branch -a

docs.title: Switch Between Branches
docs.text:
git switch feature/dark-mode    # Switch to the feature branch
git switch main                 # Switch back to main

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
While Git doesn't enforce this, teams usually follow a pattern:

feature/                 — New feature
  feature/dark-mode
  feature/user-profiles

bugfix/ or fix/          — Bug fix
  fix/login-timeout
  fix/crash-on-startup

docs/                    — Documentation
  docs/api-reference

chore/                   — Maintenance, refactoring
  chore/upgrade-dependencies

Choose a convention and stick with it. It makes history readable.

docs.title: Merging
docs.text:
When you're done with a branch and want to incorporate it into main:

Visual learner? Check Visual Guide: Merging to see what happens to the commit graph.

docs.title: Basic Merge
docs.text:
git switch main          # Switch to the branch you want to merge INTO
git merge feature/dark-mode

Git will merge the changes. If there are no conflicts, you're done.

docs.title: Merge with a Commit Message
docs.text:
git merge --no-ff feature/dark-mode

The --no-ff flag creates a merge commit even if Git could fast-forward. This preserves the branch history and makes it clear what changes were part of the feature.

docs.title: Conflicts
docs.text: When two branches change the same part of a file, Git can't automatically merge them. You have to resolve the conflict manually.

docs.title: Detecting Conflicts
docs.text:
git merge feature/dark-mode
# Output:
# CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in styles.css
# Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.

docs.title: Resolving Conflicts
docs.text:
Open the conflicted file in your editor. You'll see:

<<<<<<< HEAD
background: white;
=======
background: black;
>>>>>>> feature/dark-mode
  • <<<<<<< HEAD — Your current branch's version
  • ======= — The separator
  • >>>>>>> feature/dark-mode — The branch being merged in

Choose which version to keep (or combine them):

background: var(--background-color);  /* Supports both light and dark */

Then resolve the conflict:

git add styles.css
git commit -m "Merge feature/dark-mode (resolved conflict in styles.css)"

docs.title: Aborting a Merge
docs.text:
If the conflict is too messy, abort and try again:

git merge --abort

docs.title: Deleting Branches
docs.text:
Once a branch is merged, delete it to keep things clean:

# Delete locally
git branch -d feature/dark-mode

# Delete on remote
git push origin --delete feature/dark-mode

docs.title: Push a New Branch
docs.text:
git switch -c feature/new-feature
git add .
git commit -m "Start new feature"
git push -u origin feature/new-feature

The -u flag sets the upstream, so future git push commands default to this branch.

docs.title: Track a Remote Branch
docs.text:
If someone else created a branch on the remote and you want to work on it:

git switch feature/their-feature

Git automatically creates a local tracking branch.

docs.title: See Remote Branches
docs.text:
git branch -r

This shows all branches on the remote that you're aware of.

docs.title: Advanced: Rebasing
docs.text:
Rebasing is an alternative to merging. Instead of creating a merge commit, it replays your commits on top of another branch.

git switch feature/dark-mode
git rebase main

This takes each commit from feature/dark-mode and replays them on top of the current main. The result is a linear history without merge commits.

Warning: Don't rebase branches that other people are using. It rewrites history, which causes problems when they pull. More on this in Advanced Features.

docs.title: I Accidentally Switched Branches and Made Changes
docs.text:
git stash                          # Save your changes
git switch correct-branch
git stash pop                      # Restore your changes

docs.title: I Want to See What's Different Between Branches
docs.text:
git diff main feature/dark-mode

This shows all differences between the two branches.

docs.title: I Want to Merge But Keep My Branch History Clean
docs.text:
Use rebase instead of merge: 

git switch feature/new-feature
git rebase main
git switch main
git merge feature/new-feature

This creates a linear history: main -> rebase changes on top.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• One feature per branch — Don't mix unrelated changes
  • Keep branches short-lived — Merge back to main within a few days if possible
  • Name branches clearly — Use the convention your team follows
  • Delete merged branches — Keeps the branch list manageable
  • Pull before you push — Avoid conflicts by staying up-to-date

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Ready to learn advanced techniques? Jump to Advanced Features.

For team workflows, see Collaboration Patterns.

docs.title: 5. Advanced Features
docs.text: Master Git with these powerful techniques.

docs.title: Rebase
docs.text:
Rebasing replays your commits on top of another branch, creating a linear history instead of merge commits.

Visual learner? Check Visual Guide: Rebasing to see how commits are replayed.

docs.title: When to Use Rebase
docs.text:
• To keep history clean — Linear history is easier to read and debug
  • Before merging to main — Rebase your feature branch on main to integrate changes
  • Never on shared branches — Don't rebase branches that others depend on

docs.title: Basic Rebase
docs.text:
git switch feature/dark-mode
git rebase main

This replays each of your feature commits on top of the current main branch. If there are conflicts, Git pauses and asks you to resolve them before continuing.

docs.title: Interactive Rebase
docs.text:
Rewrite history by changing, combining, or reordering commits:

git rebase -i main

This opens an editor with a list of commits. Change pick to: - reword — Change the commit message - squash — Combine this commit with the previous one - drop — Delete the commit - edit — Stop and let you modify the commit

Example: Combine three small commits into one: 

pick 1a2b3c Fix layout bug
squash 2b3c4d Add test
squash 3c4d5e Update docs

# Results in one commit: "Fix layout bug"

docs.title: Squashing
docs.text: Squashing combines multiple commits into one. Useful before merging to keep the main branch clean.

docs.title: Squash During Merge
docs.text:
git switch main
git merge --squash feature/dark-mode
git commit -m "Add dark mode support"

This creates a single commit on main containing all the changes from the feature branch.

docs.title: Squash Using Rebase
docs.text:
git rebase -i main

Then change pick to squash for all commits except the first.

docs.title: Cherry-Pick
docs.text:
Apply a specific commit from one branch to another without merging the entire branch.

Visual learner? Check Visual Guide: Cherry-Pick to see the commit graph.

git cherry-pick abc123def

This copies the commit with hash abc123def into your current branch.

docs.title: Useful For
docs.text:
• Backporting a bugfix to an older version
  • Applying a specific change without unrelated changes
  • Recovering a commit that was accidentally deleted

docs.title: Cherry-Pick Multiple Commits
docs.text:
git cherry-pick abc123def..def456abc

This cherry-picks all commits from abc123def to def456abc.

docs.title: Stash
docs.text: Temporarily save work without committing it. Useful when you need to switch branches but aren't ready to commit.

docs.title: Save Changes
docs.text:
git stash                    # Save all changes
git stash save "wip: dark mode"   # With a description

docs.title: List Stashed Changes
docs.text:
git stash list

Output: 

stash@{0}: wip: dark mode
stash@{1}: Fix typo in header

docs.title: Restore Stashed Changes
docs.text:
git stash pop               # Restore the most recent stash
git stash pop stash@{1}     # Restore a specific stash

docs.title: Delete a Stash
docs.text:
git stash drop stash@{0}
git stash clear             # Delete all stashes

docs.title: Amending Commits
docs.text: Fix a commit after the fact (only before pushing to a shared branch).

docs.title: Change the Commit Message
docs.text:
git commit --amend --no-edit    # Keep the message
git commit --amend              # Change the message

docs.title: Add Forgotten Files
docs.text:
git add forgotten-file.txt
git commit --amend --no-edit

The file is added to the last commit.

docs.title: Change the Commit Content
docs.text:
git reset --soft HEAD~1         # Undo the commit, keep changes
git add new-changes.txt         # Add more files
git commit -m "Original message"

docs.title: Reset
docs.text: Move HEAD to a different commit. Be careful: this can discard work.

docs.title: Soft Reset
docs.text:
git reset --soft HEAD~1

Undoes the last commit but keeps your changes staged. You can now recommit differently.

docs.title: Mixed Reset (Default)
docs.text:
git reset HEAD~1

Undoes the last commit and unstages changes. Your files are unchanged.

docs.title: Hard Reset
docs.text:
git reset --hard HEAD~1

Danger! This undoes the commit AND discards all changes. Only use this if you're sure.

docs.title: Reset to a Specific Commit
docs.text:
git reset --hard abc123def

Move HEAD to commit abc123def and discard everything after. Only use on local, unpushed commits.

docs.title: Reflog
docs.text:
Git keeps a log of every movement of HEAD. Use this to recover "lost" commits.

git reflog

Output: 

abc123d HEAD@{0}: commit: Fix login bug
def456a HEAD@{1}: checkout: switching to feature-branch

Find a lost commit here and reset to it: 

git reset --hard abc123d

docs.title: Tag
docs.text: Mark important commits (e.g., releases) with a permanent label.

docs.title: Create a Tag
docs.text:
git tag v1.0.0              # Lightweight tag
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Release 1.0"  # Annotated tag (recommended)

docs.title: List Tags
docs.text:
git tag
git tag -l "v1*"            # Filter by pattern

docs.title: Push Tags
docs.text:
git push origin v1.0.0      # Push one tag
git push origin --tags      # Push all tags

docs.title: See What Changed in a Commit
docs.text:
git show abc123def

Shows the commit message and the exact changes.

docs.title: Compare Branches
docs.text:
git diff main feature/dark-mode

Shows all differences between the two branches.

docs.title: Find Who Changed a Line
docs.text:
git blame styles.css

Shows the author and commit for each line.

docs.title: Search Commit Messages
docs.text:
git log --grep="fix login"

Finds all commits mentioning "fix login".

docs.title: See Unpushed Commits
docs.text:
git log origin/main..HEAD

Shows commits you've made that aren't on the remote yet.

docs.title: See Unmerged Branches
docs.text:
git branch --no-merged

Branches that haven't been merged to the current branch.

docs.title: Create a Backup Before Risky Operations
docs.text:
git branch backup
# Now you can safely experiment, knowing backup exists
git reset --hard abc123def
git reflog  # If something goes wrong, you can recover

docs.title: Clean Up Local Branches
docs.text:
git branch --merged | grep -v main | xargs git branch -d

Deletes all merged branches except main.

docs.title: I Rebased a Shared Branch
docs.text:
If you rebased a branch others are using, don't force-push. Instead, reset it:

git reset --hard origin/branch-name

Tell your team and they'll need to pull the reset.

docs.title: I Amended a Pushed Commit
docs.text: Don't amend commits that others have pulled. If you must, warn your team they'll need to reset.

docs.title: I Cherry-Picked the Wrong Commit
docs.text:
Find the commit hash and reset: 

git reset --hard HEAD~1

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Keep commits atomic — One logical change per commit
  • Write clear messages — Explain why, not what
  • Never force-push to shared branches — It breaks teammate workflows
  • Test before pushing — Run tests after rebase/cherry-pick
  • Use reflog as a safety net — You can recover almost anything

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Ready to collaborate with your team? Jump to Collaboration Patterns.

Or dive into Fixing Mistakes for disaster recovery.

docs.title: 6. Collaboration Patterns
docs.text: Work effectively with teammates using Git.

docs.title: The Pull Request Workflow
docs.text:
Most teams use this pattern:

  1. Create a branch for your feature
  2. Make commits and push to the remote
  3. Open a Pull Request (PR) for code review
  4. Address feedback
  5. Merge when approved

docs.title: Step 1: Create a Branch
docs.text:
git switch -c feature/dark-mode

Use clear naming (see Branching & Merging for conventions).

docs.title: Step 2: Make and Push Commits
docs.text:
git add .
git commit -m "Add dark mode toggle to header"
git commit -m "Add dark mode styles"
git push -u origin feature/dark-mode

Push frequently. It's a backup and lets teammates see your progress.

docs.title: Step 3: Open a Pull Request
docs.text:
On GitHub/GitLab, create a PR from your branch to main. Include:

  • Title — One-line summary of the change
  • Description — Why the change exists, how it works, any caveats
  • Screenshots — For UI changes
  • Related issues — Link to any issues this fixes (e.g., "Fixes #123")

Example description: 

## What
Add dark mode support to the app.

## Why
Users have requested dark mode for years. This is the top feature
request on our feedback board.

## How
- New toggle in the header
- CSS variables for light/dark colors
- localStorage to persist user preference

## Testing
Tested in Chrome, Firefox, and Safari on macOS and Windows.
Dark mode toggle persists across page refreshes.

Fixes #1234

docs.title: Step 4: Code Review
docs.text:
Teammates review your code. They might request changes: - "Can you add a test for this?" - "Should we handle the error case here?" - "Let's simplify this logic"

Address feedback by: 1. Making the requested changes 2. Committing them 3. Pushing to the branch 4. Replying with what you changed

Important: Don't force-push after review. It rewrites history and makes it hard to see what changed.

docs.title: Step 5: Merge
docs.text:
Once approved, merge the PR:

# On the PR page, click "Merge"

Or command line: 

git switch main
git pull
git merge feature/dark-mode
git push

Then delete the branch: 

git branch -d feature/dark-mode
git push origin --delete feature/dark-mode

docs.title: Keep Main Stable
docs.text:
Rule: Never push directly to main. Always use branches and PRs.

This ensures: - Code is reviewed before merging - Tests pass before production - History is clean and traceable

docs.title: Rebase Feature Branches Before Merging
docs.text:
Before opening a PR or merging, rebase your branch on the latest main:

git fetch
git rebase origin/main
git push --force-with-lease origin feature/dark-mode

This integrates recent changes from main and prevents conflicts.

Note: Use --force-with-lease instead of --force. It's safer (won't overwrite remote changes you didn't expect).

docs.title: Squash Commits When Merging
docs.text:
Keep main's history clean. Instead of merging all commits:

git merge --squash feature/dark-mode
git commit -m "Add dark mode support"
git push

This creates one clean commit on main.

Alternatively, some teams use "squash and merge" on the PR page.

docs.title: Commit Hygiene
docs.text:
Write commits that tell a story:

Bad: 

Update files
Fix stuff
WIP
Finish feature

Good: 

Add dark mode toggle to header
Add CSS variables for theme colors
Persist dark mode preference to localStorage

Each commit should be a logical, self-contained change.

docs.title: Handling Merge Conflicts
docs.text: When your branch conflicts with main:

docs.title: Option 1: Rebase and Resolve
docs.text:
git fetch
git rebase origin/main

Git pauses on conflicts. Resolve them:

# Edit conflicted files
git add .
git rebase --continue

docs.title: Option 2: Merge and Resolve
docs.text:
git fetch
git merge origin/main

Resolve conflicts:

# Edit conflicted files
git add .
git commit -m "Merge main into feature branch"

docs.title: Preventing Conflicts
docs.text:
• Pull/fetch often to stay synchronized
  • Avoid editing the same files as teammates
  • Communicate about what you're working on

docs.title: For Reviewers
docs.text:
• Be constructive — "Why not use const instead?" not "This is wrong"
  • Approve when satisfied — Don't block on minor style issues
  • Ask questions — "What's the purpose of this logic?"
  • Suggest, don't demand — "We could also..." vs "You must..."
  • Review promptly — Hours, not days

docs.title: For Authors
docs.text:
• Keep PRs small — Easier to review, easier to understand
  • Explain your intent — The code shows what, the description shows why
  • Be responsive — Address feedback quickly
  • Ask for clarification — If feedback is unclear
  • Don't take it personally — Code review is about the code, not you

docs.title: Feature Branch Workflow
docs.text:
Each feature gets its own branch, reviewed before merging to main.

main (always stable)
├── feature/dark-mode
├── feature/user-profiles
└── fix/login-timeout

Best for: Most teams and projects.

docs.title: Git Flow
docs.text:
More complex branching model with develop and release branches.

main (releases only)
├── develop (integration branch)
├── feature/... (merged into develop)
└── release/... (bugfixes before release)

Best for: Large teams, scheduled releases.

docs.title: Trunk-Based Development
docs.text:
Everyone commits directly to main, feature flags control visibility.

main
├── [commit with feature flag: disabled]
├── [commit with feature flag: enabled]
└── [commit with feature flag: removed]

Best for: Continuous deployment, very communicative teams.

docs.title: Protecting Main
docs.text:
Set up branch protection rules to enforce standards:

  • Require PR reviews before merging
  • Require CI/CD tests to pass
  • Require status checks (linting, type checking)
  • Prevent force-pushing
  • Dismiss stale PR approvals when new commits are pushed

This prevents accidents and ensures quality.

docs.title: Handling Urgent Fixes
docs.text: Sometimes you need to fix a critical bug immediately.

docs.title: If It's a One-Line Fix
docs.text:
git switch main
git switch -c fix/critical-bug
git commit -m "Fix critical bug"
git push -u origin fix/critical-bug
# Open PR, get quick review, merge

docs.title: If It's Complex
docs.text: Use the same process but loop in senior developers for fast review.

docs.title: "Rebase and squash before merge"
docs.text:
git fetch
git rebase origin/main
git push --force-with-lease
# Then squash and merge on the PR page

Keeps main's history clean with one commit per feature.

docs.title: "Merge with merge commits"
docs.text:
git merge --no-ff feature/dark-mode

Preserves branch history, making it clear what changes were part of the feature.

docs.title: "Rebase on main, then merge without merge commit"
docs.text:
git rebase origin/main
git merge --ff-only

Clean linear history.

docs.title: Push Frequently
docs.text:
git push origin feature/dark-mode

This backs up your work and lets teammates see your progress.

docs.title: Fetch Before Working
docs.text:
git fetch

Update your local view of remote branches. Do this daily.

docs.title: Keep Branches Up-to-Date
docs.text:
git fetch
git rebase origin/main
git push --force-with-lease

Prevents your branch from diverging too far from main.

docs.title: Issues and Discussions
docs.text:
Link commits and PRs to issues:

In commit messages: 

Fix form validation

Fixes #123

In PR description: 

Fixes #123
Depends on #456

GitHub auto-closes issues when the PR is merged.

docs.title: When Opening a PR
docs.text:
• Tag reviewers: @teammate
  • Explain non-obvious changes
  • Link related work
  • Mention if it's urgent

docs.title: When Reviewing
docs.text:
• Comment with context, not just "LGTM"
  • Ask questions if you don't understand
  • Suggest improvements, don't demand

docs.title: When Merging
docs.text:
• Thank the reviewers
  • Celebrate the merge (for team morale)
  • Note any follow-up work needed

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• One PR = one feature — Mix concerns only when necessary
  • Keep PRs under 400 lines — Easier to review thoroughly
  • Never force-push to main — Ever
  • Require code review — Even for senior developers
  • Protect your main branch — Use GitHub/GitLab settings
  • Automate what you can — Tests, linting, formatting

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Ready for disaster recovery? Jump to Fixing Mistakes.

Or check out Command Reference for a quick lookup.

docs.title: 7. Fixing Mistakes
docs.text: Recover from Git errors. The good news: almost nothing is permanent in Git.

docs.title: I Made Changes I Don't Want
Haven't Staged Yet
docs.text:
git restore filename.txt        # Discard changes to one file
git restore .                   # Discard all changes

Or the older syntax: 

git checkout -- filename.txt

docs.title: Already Staged (But Not Committed)
docs.text:
git restore --staged filename.txt   # Unstage the file
git restore filename.txt            # Also discard the changes

Or: 

git reset filename.txt              # Unstage
git restore filename.txt            # Discard

docs.title: Already Committed (But Not Pushed)
docs.text:
git reset --soft HEAD~1         # Undo commit, keep changes staged
git reset --mixed HEAD~1        # Undo commit, keep changes unstaged
git reset --hard HEAD~1         # Undo commit, discard changes (dangerous!)

For older commits: 

git reset --hard abc123def      # Go back to that commit, discard everything after

docs.title: I Committed to the Wrong Branch
docs.text:
You committed to main but meant to commit to feature/dark-mode.

# Get the commit hash
git log

# Reset main to before your commit
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Switch to the correct branch
git switch feature/dark-mode

# Create the commit there
git add .
git commit -m "Your commit message"

# Go back to main and verify it's clean
git switch main
git status  # Should be clean

docs.title: I Need to Undo My Last Commit Message
docs.text:
git commit --amend              # Opens editor to change message

Or change message and keep everything: 

git commit --amend -m "New message"

Only do this before pushing. After pushing, it creates problems for teammates.

docs.title: I Accidentally Deleted a Branch
docs.text:
Git keeps a log of everything. Recover with reflog:

git reflog

Find the branch's last commit: 

abc123d HEAD@{0}: commit: Feature complete
def456a HEAD@{1}: checkout: switching to feature

Recreate the branch: 

git switch -c feature/dark-mode abc123d

docs.title: I Pushed Something I Shouldn't Have
If No One Else Has Pulled Yet
docs.text:
git reset --hard HEAD~1         # Undo locally
git push --force-with-lease     # Overwrite remote

Warn your team first. Then tell them to pull the reset.

docs.title: If Others Have Already Pulled
docs.text:
Use git revert instead of reset (creates a new commit that undoes the change):

git revert HEAD                 # Undo the last commit
git push

Now everyone pulls the revert commit.

docs.title: I Lost a Commit
docs.text:
Git keeps a log of HEAD movements. Find it:

git reflog

Example output: 

abc123d HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~1
def456a HEAD@{1}: commit: Important feature

Recover the commit: 

git reset --hard def456a

Or create a new branch from it: 

git switch -c recovery def456a

docs.title: I Have a Merge Conflict
docs.text:
See Branching & Merging for detailed resolution.

Quick version: 1. Open conflicted files 2. Resolve conflicts (remove <<<<, ====, >>>>) 3. Stage the files: git add . 4. Continue: git commit or git rebase --continue (depending on what you were doing)

docs.title: Abort the Merge/Rebase
docs.text:
If it's too messy: 

git merge --abort
git rebase --abort

Try again later.

docs.title: My Repo Seems Corrupted
docs.text:
Try to check the repo's integrity:

git fsck --full

If it finds issues, you can usually recover from backups or remote copies.

If you have a remote copy: 

git fetch origin
git reset --hard origin/main

docs.title: I Accidentally Rebased the Wrong Branch
docs.text:
If you rebased a shared branch and pushed it:

  1. Don't panic — you can fix this
  2. Tell your team
  3. Reset the branch: git reset --hard origin/branch-name
  4. Tell everyone to pull the reset

If you rebased locally but haven't pushed: 

git reset --hard origin/main    # Go back to remote state
git reflog                      # Find where you were before
git reset --hard abc123d        # Go back to that state

docs.title: Common "I Messed Up" Commands
docs.text: Situation Command Discard changes to a file git restore filename.txt Undo the last commit git reset --soft HEAD~1 Undo and discard changes git reset --hard HEAD~1 See what you did git reflog Recover a lost commit git reset --hard <commit> Fix a commit message git commit --amend Undo a pushed commit git revert <commit> Abort a merge git merge --abort Abort a rebase git rebase --abort

docs.title: The Nuclear Option
docs.text:
If everything is a mess, you can clone the remote repo fresh:

cd ..
rm -rf messed-up-repo
git clone <url> messed-up-repo
cd messed-up-repo

This is extreme but sometimes necessary.

docs.title: What You Can't Undo
docs.text:
Almost nothing is permanent in Git, but here are the rare cases:

  • Garbage collected commits — If a commit isn't referenced anywhere and hasn't been pushed, it might be garbage collected after ~30 days. Keep backups of important work.
  • Force-pushed to a remote where someone else started new work — If you force-push and someone else pulled the old state, they have work on the old state. This is messy but recoverable.

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
Avoid these mistakes by:

  • Commit frequently — More checkpoints mean less lost work
  • Use feature branches — Never work directly on main
  • Pull before you push — Avoid conflicts
  • Test before pushing — Don't push broken code
  • Read git status — Know what you're committing
  • Use --force-with-lease — Safer than --force

docs.title: Get Help
docs.text:
If you're stuck:

git status              # Current state
git log --oneline       # Recent commits
git reflog              # Everything you've done

And remember: almost nothing is permanent. Take a breath and work through it.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Check out Command Reference for a full command lookup.

Or go back to Advanced Features to learn techniques for preventing mistakes.

docs.title: 8. Command Reference
docs.text: Quick lookup for common Git commands.

docs.title: Setup & Configuration
docs.text:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "email@example.com"
git config --global --list              # View all settings

docs.title: Creating & Cloning
docs.text:
git init                        # Initialize a new repo
git clone <url>                 # Clone an existing repo
git clone <url> folder-name     # Clone into a specific folder

docs.title: Checking Status
docs.text:
git status                      # Current state
git status -s                   # Short format
git log                         # Commit history
git log --oneline               # Condensed history
git log -p                      # Show changes in each commit
git log --graph --oneline --all # Visual branch history
git diff                        # Unstaged changes
git diff --staged               # Staged changes
git show <commit>               # Show a specific commit
git blame <file>                # Who changed each line

docs.title: Making Changes
docs.text:
git add <file>                  # Stage a file
git add .                       # Stage all changes
git add -p                      # Interactive staging
git commit -m "message"         # Commit with message
git commit                      # Commit, open editor for message
git commit --amend              # Modify the last commit
git commit --amend --no-edit    # Add to last commit, keep message
git restore <file>              # Discard changes to a file
git restore .                   # Discard all changes
git restore --staged <file>     # Unstage a file

docs.title: Branching
docs.text:
git branch                      # List local branches
git branch -a                   # List all branches (local + remote)
git branch <name>               # Create a branch
git branch -d <name>            # Delete a branch
git switch <branch>             # Switch to a branch
git switch -c <branch>          # Create and switch to a branch
git checkout <branch>           # Old syntax for switching
git checkout -b <branch>        # Old syntax for create + switch

docs.title: Merging
docs.text:
git merge <branch>              # Merge a branch into current
git merge --no-ff <branch>      # Merge with merge commit
git merge --squash <branch>     # Squash commits before merge
git merge --abort               # Cancel a merge in progress

docs.title: Rebasing
docs.text:
git rebase <branch>             # Rebase current branch onto another
git rebase -i <branch>          # Interactive rebase
git rebase --continue           # Continue after resolving conflicts
git rebase --abort              # Cancel a rebase

docs.title: Remote Operations
docs.text:
git remote                      # List remotes
git remote -v                   # Show remote URLs
git remote add origin <url>     # Add a remote
git fetch                       # Download from remote
git fetch <remote>              # Fetch from specific remote
git pull                        # Fetch and merge from remote
git push                        # Push to remote
git push -u origin <branch>     # Push and set upstream
git push <remote> <branch>      # Push to specific remote
git push --force-with-lease     # Force push (safer)
git push origin --delete <branch>  # Delete remote branch
git push --tags                 # Push all tags

docs.title: Undoing Changes
docs.text:
git restore <file>              # Discard unstaged changes
git restore --staged <file>     # Unstage
git reset HEAD~1                # Undo last commit (mixed)
git reset --soft HEAD~1         # Undo, keep changes staged
git reset --hard HEAD~1         # Undo, discard changes
git reset --hard <commit>       # Go back to a specific commit
git revert <commit>             # Undo a commit (creates new commit)
git checkout -- <file>          # Old syntax for discard

docs.title: Cherry-Pick & Stash
docs.text:
git cherry-pick <commit>        # Apply a specific commit
git cherry-pick <commit1>..<commit2>  # Multiple commits
git cherry-pick --abort         # Cancel cherry-pick
git stash                       # Save changes temporarily
git stash save "message"        # Stash with description
git stash list                  # List stashes
git stash pop                   # Restore most recent stash
git stash pop stash@{0}         # Restore specific stash
git stash drop stash@{0}        # Delete a stash
git stash clear                 # Delete all stashes

docs.title: Tags
docs.text:
git tag                         # List tags
git tag <name>                  # Create lightweight tag
git tag -a <name> -m "message"  # Create annotated tag
git show <tag>                  # Show tag details
git push origin <tag>           # Push one tag
git push origin --tags          # Push all tags
git tag -d <name>               # Delete local tag
git push origin --delete <tag>  # Delete remote tag

docs.title: Inspecting Changes
docs.text:
git diff                        # Show unstaged changes
git diff <branch1> <branch2>    # Compare two branches
git diff <commit1> <commit2>    # Compare two commits
git log --grep="text"           # Search commit messages
git log -S "code"               # Search for code changes
git log --since="2 weeks ago"   # Commits in last 2 weeks
git log <file>                  # History of a file
git blame <file>                # Show who changed each line

docs.title: Cleaning Up
docs.text:
git clean -fd                   # Remove untracked files and dirs
git gc                          # Clean up and optimize repo
git reflog                      # Show all HEAD movements
git fsck --full                 # Check repo integrity

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text:
git rebase -i HEAD~3            # Rebase last 3 commits
git squash <commit>             # Interactive rebase (see 05-advanced.md)
git bisect start                # Start binary search for bad commit
git filter-branch               # Rewrite history (advanced)
git worktree add <path>         # Create multiple working directories

docs.title: Useful Aliases
docs.text:
Add these to your Git config for faster commands:

git config --global alias.st status
git config --global alias.co checkout
git config --global alias.br branch
git config --global alias.ci commit
git config --global alias.unstage 'restore --staged'
git config --global alias.last 'log -1 HEAD'
git config --global alias.visual 'log --graph --oneline --all'

Then use: 

git st              # instead of git status
git co main         # instead of git checkout main

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
git help <command>              # Opens man page
git <command> --help            # Quick help
git                             # List basic commands
git config --help               # Help for config

docs.title: "I want to see what changed"
docs.text:
git status              # Current state
git diff                # What I changed
git log --oneline       # Commit history
git show <commit>       # Details of a commit

docs.title: "I want to save my work"
docs.text:
git add .               # Stage everything
git commit -m "msg"     # Commit
git push                # Push to remote

docs.title: "I want to update from remote"
docs.text:
git fetch               # See what's new
git pull                # Fetch and merge

docs.title: "I messed up"
docs.text:
git status              # See the mess
git reflog              # Find where you were
git reset --hard <commit>  # Go back

docs.title: "I want to work on something new"
docs.text:
git switch -c feature/name      # Create branch
git push -u origin feature/name # Push it

docs.title: "I want to merge my changes"
docs.text:
git switch main         # Go to main
git merge feature/name  # Merge the branch
git push                # Push to remote

docs.title: Exit Git Commands
docs.text: Some commands open an editor or pager. Exit with: - Vim/Vi — :q then Enter - Nano — Ctrl+X - Less pager — q

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
• Repository — A Git project folder
  • Commit — A snapshot of changes
  • Branch — A line of development
  • Remote — A server copy (GitHub, etc.)
  • Staging — Preparing changes for commit
  • HEAD — Current commit you're on
  • Index — Staging area
  • Merge — Combine branches
  • Rebase — Replay commits on a new base
  • Cherry-pick — Apply specific commit
  • Stash — Temporarily save changes
  • Tag — Mark important commits
  • Reflog — History of HEAD movements

For detailed explanations, see the other guides: - Concepts — What is Git? - Basics — Day-to-day commands - Branching — Working with branches - Advanced — Power user features - Collaboration — Team workflows - Troubleshooting — Fixing mistakes

docs.title: 9. Visual Guide: Git Tree Progression
docs.text: See how the commit graph evolves as you perform Git operations.

docs.title: Initial Repository
docs.text:
Your first commit creates a single node in the graph:

C1 ← main

docs.title: Linear History
docs.text: As you commit changes, the chain grows:

docs.title: Creating a Branch
docs.text:
When you create a branch, it's just a pointer to the current commit:

Both branches point to C3. When you commit on the branch, it advances while main stays behind. The branches have diverged. C3 is their common ancestor.

docs.title: Merging (Fast-Forward)
docs.text:
If main hasn't changed since you branched, Git can fast-forward:

The main branch pointer moves forward to C5. No new commit is created.

docs.title: Merging (Non-Fast-Forward)
docs.text:
If main has new commits, a merge commit is created:

A merge commit M (shown in red) has two parents: C6 and C5. Both branches now point to M.

docs.title: Rebasing
docs.text:
Rebasing replays your commits on top of another branch:

C4 and C5 are replayed as C4' and C5' (shown in purple) on top of C6. They have new hashes because their parent changed. The old C4 and C5 are no longer referenced (can be garbage collected).

docs.title: Result After Rebase + Fast-Forward Merge
docs.text: Linear history, clean commit graph.

docs.title: Cherry-Pick
docs.text:
Cherry-pick copies a specific commit onto another branch:

C4 is copied as C4' (new hash, shown in cyan) onto main. Both branches still exist unchanged. The original C4 and C5 remain on the feature branch.

docs.title: Detached HEAD
docs.text:
When you checkout a specific commit instead of a branch, HEAD points directly to the commit:

If you make new commits in detached HEAD, they're not on any branch. For example:

C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4 ← main
     ↑
     C5 ← C6 ← HEAD (detached)

If you switch away without creating a branch, C5 and C6 become unreferenced (but recoverable via reflog).

docs.title: Multiple Branches
docs.text:
Complex projects often have multiple feature branches:

Each branch has its own commit history. main is the integration point.

docs.title: Merge Conflicts
docs.text:
When branches modify the same lines, a merge conflict occurs:

         C4 ← C5 ← feature/auth
        ↙
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C6 ← main
(both modified styles.css)

During merge:
         C4 ← C5 ← feature/auth
        ↙
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C6 ← ??? (conflict!)

You resolve the conflict manually, then create a merge commit:

         C4 ← C5 ← feature/auth
        ↙        ↘
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C6 ← M ← main

docs.title: Undoing Commits (Reset)
docs.text:
Reset moves the branch pointer back:

Before: git reset --hard HEAD~2
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4 ← C5 ← main
                          ↑
                        HEAD

After:
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← main, HEAD
     ↑
(C4 and C5 still exist, just unreferenced)

The commits are discarded from the branch perspective but recoverable via reflog.

docs.title: Reverting Commits
docs.text:
Revert creates a new commit that undoes a previous one:

Before: git revert C3
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4 ← C5 ← main

After:
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4 ← C5 ← R ← main
(R's changes are the opposite of C3)

The original commits stay in history. R is a normal new commit.

docs.title: Amending the Last Commit
docs.text:
Amend modifies the most recent commit:

Before: git commit --amend
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4 ← main

After:
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4' ← main
(C4 is replaced by C4' with new hash)

If C4 hasn't been pushed, only your local history changes. If it's been pushed, you'll need to force-push (dangerous!).

docs.title: Push and Fetch
docs.text:
When you push, the remote's commit graph updates:

Local:
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4 ← main

Remote (before push):
C1 ← C2 ← main

After: git push origin main
Local:
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4 ← main

Remote:
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4 ← main

Both are now synchronized. Remote tracking branches update when you fetch:

Local (after fetch):
C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4 ← main
          ↑
       origin/main

docs.title: A Real Workflow
docs.text:
Here's how a typical team workflow evolves:

1. Start: Everyone on main 

C1 ← main, origin/main

2. Alice creates feature/auth: 

           C2 ← feature/auth
          ↙
C1 ← main, origin/main

3. Bob creates feature/search: 

           C2 ← feature/auth
          ↙
C1 ← main, origin/main
  ↘
   C3 ← feature/search

4. Alice commits: 

           C2 ← C4 ← feature/auth
          ↙
C1 ← main, origin/main
  ↘
   C3 ← feature/search

5. Alice's PR is approved, merged to main: 

           C2 ← C4 ⟲
          ↙        ↘
C1 ← main, origin/main ← M
  ↘
   C3 ← feature/search

6. Bob fetches and sees main updated: 

           C2 ← C4 ⟲
          ↙        ↘
C1 ←  M ← main
↑    ↑
origin/main (before fetch)

After fetch:
           C2 ← C4 ⟲
          ↙        ↘
C1 ← origin/main ← M ← main
  ↘
   C3 ← feature/search

7. Bob rebases feature/search on the updated main: 

           C2 ← C4 ⟲
          ↙        ↘
C1 ← origin/main ← M ← main
              ↘
               C3' ← feature/search

8. Bob's PR is approved and merged: 

           C2 ← C4 ⟲
          ↙        ↘
C1 ← origin/main ← M ← C3' ⟲
                    ↑    ↘
                   main ← M2

And the cycle continues...

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Commits form a DAG — Each commit points to its parent(s)
  • Branches are pointers — Moving a branch pointer doesn't change commits
  • Merges create branches — Merge commits have multiple parents
  • Rebasing replays commits — Creates new commits with different hashes
  • History is immutable — Old commits stay accessible via reflog
  • Conflicts happen when — Two branches modify the same lines

docs.title: Visual Patterns to Remember
docs.text:
Linear history:          C1 ← C2 ← C3 ← C4

Branching:               C3 ← C4 ← feature
                        ↙
                       C1 ← C2 ← main

Merging:                C3 ← C4 ⟲
                       ↙        ↘
                       C1 ← C2 ← M ← main

Rebasing:              C1 ← C2 ← C3' ← C4' ← feature
                              ↑
                            main

These patterns repeat in every Git workflow. Understanding them helps you predict what your commit graph will look like after each operation.
